symbols: bow and arrows — the agreed signal between Jonathan and David, the instrument of the secret message
Jonathan's protective stance — the loyal friend who risked his father's wrath to save David's life
young David collecting arrows — the future king in his season of hiding and vulnerability
sunset landscape — the poignant beauty of a friendship about to be tested by separation
trees framing the scene — the field where the covenant was sealed and the arrows shot
description: A vertical composition showing two figures in a landscape setting framed by large trees. The larger figure, a tall, muscular young man in an embroidered tunic and draped robe, stands prominently at center, his left arm raised to shield his eyes as he looks into the distance. He carries a quiver of arrows and wears sandals. Beside and slightly behind him, a smaller, younger figure — a boy with curly hair — bends to gather arrows from the ground or tends to the bow. The background shows a distant mountainous landscape with a warm sunset sky.
theological_description: This painting depicts the moment from 1 Samuel 20 when Jonathan, son of King Saul, sends a secret signal to his beloved friend David using arrows shot into a field. Jonathan and David had made a covenant of friendship (1 Samuel 18:3), and when Saul's murderous jealousy toward David became evident, Jonathan devised a plan: he would shoot arrows near a stone and send a boy to collect them. If he called out 'the arrows are on this side of you,' David was safe; if 'the arrows are beyond you,' David must flee. The scene captures one of the most profound friendships in Scripture — Jonathan loved David 'as his own soul' (1 Samuel 18:1), and this love led him to sacrifice his own claim to the throne in recognition of God's anointing of David. Jonathan's loyalty embodies the proverb: 'A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity' (Proverbs 17:17). Their covenant foreshadows the new covenant in Christ, where self-sacrificing love supersedes all earthly claims and loyalties. Jonathan, the crown prince who freely yielded his succession to God's chosen one, is a type of John the Baptist, who said of Jesus, 'He must increase, but I must decrease' (John 3:30). The arrows — instruments of war turned into instruments of salvation — carry the message that preserves the life of God's anointed king.

scripture_refs.reason: The narrative of Jonathan's arrow signal to warn David of Saul's intent to kill him
The covenant of friendship between Jonathan and David, the foundation of the scene depicted

Jonathan strengthening David's hand in God, their covenant renewed in David's hiding
Jonathan yielding his royal claim to God's anointed parallels the Baptist's self-effacement before Christ
